title: Push Notifications
alt: NOTIFICATION: Now dismissing a head of the Notification Hydra… NOTIFICATION: Success! You have dismissed a head of the Notification Hydra!
transcript:
[Cueball sits at a desk in an office chair. He is typing on a computer.]
[Gray message boxes with a small circled “x” at the top right corner are shown:]
Happy April 1st (observed)!
To celebrate, we were excited to introduce a new xkcd.com feature: push notifications for new comics!
Unfortunately, this feature has gone horribly wrong.
Until further notice, we are asking people NOT to sign up for new comic notifications.
Frankly, we recommend not even clicking on any notifications to learn more.
Click this notification to learn more! (a circled arrow is shown)

“Push notifications for new comics” sounds like a simple feature, right?
There’s a nice API for browser notifications. xkcd updates three times a week.
So you just send a notification for each update, right?
That’s what we thought, too.
Click to continue! (a circled arrow is shown)

[To the side of the comic frame is a big, horizontal hexagonal stop sign]
Sign: Emergency STOP
Below the sign: Halt ALL notifications and forget everything
The good news is that we did build a button to stop xkcd new comic notifications.
You can press this button at any time and the system will stop sending you notifications.
The nightmare will be over and the server will forget you ever existed.
This part definitely works.
The rest of the system does NOT work. (a circled arrow is shown)

We’re sorry.
We don’t know how things went so wrong.
The system is sending more than three notifications a week. A LOT more.
We cannot recommend signing up for xkcd new comic notifications at this time.
Click here to ignore the warnings. (a circled arrow is shown)

No, really.
Our notification system may send a large number of very real system notifications.
These may flood your browser, displace other notifications, and cause problems.
⚠️DO NOT ENABLE XKCD NEW COMIC NOTIFICATIONS⚠️
Yeah, I get it, but I definitely want to enable xkcd new comic notifications. (a circled arrow is shown)

Are you sure?
Yes! (a circled arrow is shown)
Absolutely sure?
YES!! (a circled arrow is shown)
To enable push notifications on mobile you need to add xkcd.com to your home screen.

Click here to subscribe to xkcd notifications

[Initial static image shows Cueball sat on an office chair at a desk using a laptop computer. A notification 'window' is speech-bubbled above the computer]
[Grey notification box, header:] April 1st (Observed)
[Notification box, further text:] Open xkcd.com to view.
Laptop: [Warning sign.]

[When viewed live, various xkcd-style popover notifications appear, each can be dismissed or (usually the last on every page) invited to press an 'onwards'-style button.]

[First page of messages]
Happy April 1st! To celebrate, we were excited to introduce a new xkcd.com feature: Push Notifications for new comics!
Unfortunately, this feature has gone horribly wrong.
Until further notice, we are asking people NOT to sign up for new comic notifications.
Frankly, we recommend not even clicking on any notifications to learn more.
[Notification with 'onwards button':] Click on this notification to learn more

[Upon choosing to continue, further messages appear, replacing any prior ones left open]
"Push Notifications for new comics" sounds like a simple feature, right?
There's a nice API for browser notifications, xkcd updates three times a week.
So you just send a notification for each upafte, right?
That's what we thought, too.
[Notification with 'onwards button':] Click on this notification to continue

[Next set of popover messages]
The good news is that we did build a button to STOP xkcd new comic notifications.
You can press this button at any time and the system will stop sending you notifications.
The nightmare will be over and the server will forget you ever existed.
This part definitely works.
[Notification with 'onwards':] The rest of the system does not work.
[To the right of the comic frame, a red, octagonal button has white text upon it:]Emergency Stop
[Below the button is red text:] Halt all notifications and forget everything

[Next set of popovers, "stop" button remains permanently so long as you continue]
We're sorry.
We didn't know how things went so wrong.
The system is sending more than three notifications at this time.
['Onwards' notification:] Click here to ignore the warnings

[Next set.]
No, really.
Our notification system may send a large amount of very real system notifications.
These may flood your browser, displace other notifications, and cause problems.
[Text bookended by warning triangles:] Do not enable xkcd new comic notifications
['Onwards':] Yeah, I get it, but I definitely want to enable xkcd new comic notifications

[Just two popovers, initially]
Are you sure?
['Onwards':] Yes
[On clicking onwards, two more appear below]
Absolutely sure?
['Onwards':] Yes!!
[On clicking, a further popover]
[Red-tinted 'onwards'-style popover with warnings:] Click here to subscribe to xkcd notifications.

[...something appeared then dissappeared...]
[Genuine(?) browser dialogue activated:] xkcd.com wants to send you notifications. Block / Allow

[If allowed, one more popover in the original style]
Success!!
[Below the Emergency Stop button, an oval gray button appears labeled in white:] Silence notifications at a cost
[Below that, in grey text:]Temporarily pause your notifications at the cost of notifying two random people.

[Further changes include the contents of the comic. The transcript for these changes is available on the Comic images section.]
explanation:
The comic uses  to change the comic image over time and make other statements. Upon viewing the comic for the first time, a sequence of notifications will be rendered over the comic itself. The notifications have a fade-in animation and appear one after another. The sequence is divided into several "groups" of notifications, each containing up to 4 regular notifications and an interactable button, which has a circled arrow icon to the right as well as a different text color. Clicking the arrow removes all notifications on screen and proceeds the sequence to the next group.

At the end of the sequence, a red-background option to sign up for xkcd notifications is presented. This part differs depending on if the user is "playing" from a mobile or desktop browser.

* On desktop, clicking this will result in the browser prompting the user to allow or block xkcd.com's request to send push notifications. By allowing, the game begins. If blocked, the comic shows a message "An actual error occurred ", and the laptop bursts into flames (see the image in the table below). It also blows up in response to any other browser error, such as a 500 Internal Server Error, which is reported to happen if you click the pause button too quickly.

* On mobile, a new on-screen notification will show up, telling the user to add zk to their home screen in order for it to send push notifications on mobile. Upon doing so and opening the webpage from the home screen, the system will prompt the user to allow or block push notifications from this webpage. The outcomes of allowing and denying are the same as on desktop.

Once the game begins, you are provided with two buttons: one labeled "Emergency Stop", and one labeled "Silence notifications at a cost". Clicking the "Emergency Stop" button allows you to either restart the full game or to subscribe just to thrice-weekly notifications for new xkcd comics, while the second button will temporarily silence notifications, supposedly at the cost of notifying two random people.

The title text references the , a multi-headed serpent-like monster from Greek mythology; the Greek hero  was tasked with slaying the Hydra as . When he cut off one of its heads, however, two heads would grow in its place, resulting in more heads than before; Heracles soon realized he could not kill the Hydra in this fashion. It describes a similar situation with the "Notification Hydra": a push notification "head" of the Notification Hydra was dismissed, which subsequently spawned two more push notification heads providing updates about the status of the dismissal. If one of these new notifications were to be dismissed, it would again spawn another set of the same two push notification heads; thus, the Notification Hydra is unkillable in a manner similar to the original.

Up to 7 clickable cats periodically spawn at predetermined positions around the page, including one on the edge of the Emergency Stop button. The subdirectory where the images and scripts for this comic are stored is titled 'marconi,' which is likely a reference to the Italian engineer and inventor of the same name, , who is credited with inventing the radio, and did much work in the field of early communication systems.

===Notifications===


A general list of notification types include:
* The "Zoom notifications" only include numeric coordinates, sometimes prefixed with "oh look!". Plotting the normal coordinates creates an image of a turtle, a reference to 1416: Pixels, while the coordinates prefixed with "oh look!" create a squirrel behind the turtle. See the image above.

* A few starter notifications about this comic, the successful sending of a notification of this comic, and the lack of another comic.

* An announcement that an old comic was posted, specifying a (seemingly) random comic and its publication date. The publication date seems to be shown in a random .

* A word game where a first notification asks you to type a word, with further notifications apparently to be sent whenever someone chooses the same word as you.
** The word can be chosen by clicking on the first notification, which lands you in the comic page. The comic pane will be changed to a comic-style input dialogue with a space for the word and a submit button. Upon providing a word, a notification informs you that you will then receive notifications (of the form "Someone else has selected the word <Your Word>!"), and that your choice is permanent and cannot be changed. You can also submit a blank text field, thus receiving "Someone else has selected the word !" notifications.

* A "streak" notification counting the number of times you've clicked it. To start with, the message changes every 10 clicks; then every 50 clicks (after 100) and then at increasing intervals. After every 50 clicks there is an offer to make future clicks count twice, making this a simple version of . If you choose to accept the offer by clicking on it, it will reset your clicks back to zero but makes all future clicks count double of the current click rate; you can also reject the offer by continuing to click on the click counter message. There are occasionally notifications encouraging you to keep clicking and "extend your streak," tempting you with "a free click to keep you going". After 400 clicks it will reset your clicks back to zero but makes all future clicks count four times.
** Early notifications encourage further clicking.
** Approaching a hundred, the messages grow more concerned and later start warning that the server will crash or has crashed.
** Above 750: "Are you just doing this to annoy me?"
** 1000-2000: "You're just doing this to annoy you?"
** Above 2000: "I guess it worked"
** Above 1000000: "Wow, you're really into this"

* "Constant reminders" with references to mathematical, physical, or some other constants. The constant values are stated to be "at the tone", likely in reference to , which are live or recorded human voice services that tell you the exact time "at the third stroke" over the phone.  (In at least some portions of the USA, the wording did use "tone" rather than "strike."  For example, "At the tone, the time will be 2:32pm, and 10 seconds ... (beep)")

* A "have you seen my cat?" notification which causes all the cats to spawn when clicked. 

* Cat fact notifications that appear when a cat is clicked, in reference to this exchange.

* Various "Erroneous Alerts" with a warning triangle  (⚠ U+26A0), some with just flavor text, some corresponding to changes in the comic image.

* A notification that offers to provide weather alerts for your location, which leads into a series of notifications asking whether you live in a named city. If none of the cities that it knows are selected, your location is set to the summit of .

* When the time is near a full hour or half-hour, that the current time is five o'clock.

* An invitation to subscribe to the "What if?" YouTube channel

* A notification with the heading "System Log" that reads (INFO): A user has paused notifications
** This notification presumably appears when a random user has pressed the "Silence notifications at a cost" button.

* A " Line Feed", referencing the Windows standard of storing line returns as a carriage return character followed by a line feed (newline) character. However, the xkcd Carriage Return Line Feed is instead a news feed concerning the next stop on a supposed "Carriage Return Line" of a train system. The announcements are all related to stations on the , including the use of the phrase "".

* A reminder to comment and subscribe. When clicked, they redirect the user to one of the following Wikipedia Talk pages:
